title: Centre of mass of discrete particles

concepts.title: Centre of mass for two bodies
concepts.conceptBody:
Centre of mass for two bodies

For two masses ${{\text{x}}_{\text{cm}}}=\frac{{{\text{m}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}\times {{\text{x}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}}$

If you are making measurements from the centre of mass point for a two-mass system then the centre of mass condition can be expressed as

${{\text{m}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}_{1}}={{\text{m}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}={{\text{m}}_{2}}\frac{{{\text{x}}_{2}}}{{{\text{x}}_{1}}}$

where ${{\text{x}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{x}}_{2}}$ locate the masses. The centre of mass lies on the line connecting the two masses.

Example 1 : 

Two particles of mass 1 kg and 2 kg are located at x = 0 and x = 3m. Find the position of their centre of mass. 

Solution : 

Since, both the particles lie on x-axis, the COM will also lie on x-axis. Let the COM is located at $\text{x}=\text{x}$, then

${{\text{r}}_{1}}=$ distance of COM from the particle of mass $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ kg}=\text{x}$

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{r}}_{2}}=$ distance of COM from the particle of mass $2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ kg}$

$=\left( 3-\text{x} \right)$

Using $\frac{{{\text{r}}_{1}}}{{{\text{r}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{m}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{x}{3-x}=\frac{2}{1}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }x=2m\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Ans.

concepts.title: Centre of mass for particles
concepts.conceptBody:
Centre of Mass for Particles 

The centre of mass is the point at which all the mass can be considered to be "concentrated" for the purpose of calculating the "first moment", i.e., mass times distance. For two masses this distance is calculated from

For the more general collection of $\text{N}$ particles this becomes

\(\mathrm{Mx}_{\mathrm{cm}}=\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}\) where \(\mathrm{M}=\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{i}}=\) total mass

and when extended to three dimensions :

${{x}_{cm}}=\frac{\mathop{\sum }_{i=1}^{N}{{m}_{i}}{{x}_{i}}}{M}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{y}_{cm}}=\frac{\mathop{\sum }_{i=1}^{N}{{m}_{i}}{{y}_{i}}}{M}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{z}_{cm}}=\frac{\mathop{\sum }_{i=1}^{N}{{m}_{i}}{{z}_{i}}}{M}$

This approach applies to decreet masses even if they are not point masses if the position ${{\text{x}}_{\text{i}}}$ is taken to be the position of the centre of mass of the ${{i}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ mass. It also points the way toward the calculation of the centre of mass of an extended object. 

Example

The position vector of three particles of mass and are $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_1=(\tilde{i}+4 \tilde{j}+\tilde{k}) m, \vec{r}_2=(\tilde{i}+\tilde{j}+\tilde{k}) m$and ${{\vec{r}}_{3}}=\left( 2\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,-\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,-2\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\, \right)m$ respectively. Find the position vector of their centre of mass. 

Solution : 

The position vector of COM of the three particles will be given by

${{\vec{r}}_{\text{COM}}}=\frac{{{\text{m}}_{1}}{{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}+{{\text{m}}_{3}}{{{\vec{r}}}_{3}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}+{{\text{m}}_{3}}}$

Substituting the values, we get

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{COM}}=\frac{(1)(\tilde{\mathrm{i}}+4 \tilde{\mathrm{j}}+\tilde{\mathrm{k}})+(2)(\tilde{\mathrm{i}}+\tilde{\mathrm{j}}+\tilde{\mathrm{k}})+3(2 \tilde{\mathrm{i}}-\tilde{\mathrm{j}}-2 \tilde{\mathrm{k}})}{1+2+3}=\frac{9 \tilde{\mathrm{i}}+3 \tilde{\mathrm{j}}-3 \tilde{\mathrm{k}}}{6}$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{COM}}=\frac{1}{2}(3 \tilde{\mathrm{i}}+\tilde{\mathrm{j}}-\tilde{\mathrm{k}}) \mathrm{m}$ Ans.

examples.title: The centre of mass of a system particles does not depend on :
The centre of mass of a system of two particles divides. The distance between them is
Two particles of masses are in the ratio $1: 2$. The ratio of distance of the center of mass of the system of these two particles from the lighter particle to that from the heavier particle is

Two spheres of mass $\mathrm{M}$ and $7 \mathrm{M}$ are connected by a rod whose mass is negligible, and the distance between the centres of each sphere is $\mathrm{d}$. How far from the centre of the $7 \mathrm{M}$ sphere is the Centre of Mass for this object?
